source_title: You Can’t Hurt David Goggins: Going Beyond Motivation & Why Mindset Is Everything

paragraphs.speaker: rich
paragraphs.name: Rich Roll
paragraphs.text: David Goggins returns for his second podcast appearance, exactly two years after his first New Year's Day 2016 conversation—by far the most popular episode in this show's history. His new memoir, Can't Hurt Me, just came out. This book holds the potential to change your life or, at minimum, reframe your personal sense of capability. The audiobook version, read by co-writer Adam Skolnick, includes off-the-cuff podcast conversations after every chapter that are utterly compelling.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Goggins
paragraphs.text: You must go to war with yourself before you find peace. So I'm trying to give you tools on how to do that. It's a hard journey. It's a real journey. It's a journey that's gonna take you way outside of being comfortable. The most important conversation is the one you have with yourself. You wake up with it, you walk around with it, you go to bed with it. You got to change the internal dialogue.

paragraphs.speaker: rich
paragraphs.name: Rich Roll
paragraphs.text: David's decision to self-publish Can't Hurt Me is generating huge sales—number two or three on Amazon of all books—without any mainstream media whatsoever. No Today Show, no Good Morning America. This is all self-propelled DIY. There's got to be a lot of people in publishing looking at that going, what is going on here?

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Goggins
paragraphs.text: I wanted to own 100% of the suffering I went through in my life. I don't want anybody telling me, hey, Goggins, you can't say this. I suffered this badly and you're going to tell me I can't say this? I made sure that I wanted to own all of Can't Hurt Me and own all of my own life story. Everything I do, I try to do my very best. I want to be so real and so authentic to the point where it was embarrassing to me.

paragraphs.speaker: rich
paragraphs.name: Rich Roll
paragraphs.text: Writing this book was the hardest thing you've ever done. As somebody who's done a lot of hard shit, that was a surprising thing to hear. You refer to yourself in the third person always, like this character you're trying to aspire to be that's distinct from you.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Goggins
paragraphs.text: I'm amazed at what a human being can actually do. When I start talking to myself in the third person, it's strictly because I know that I literally made this human being out of a whole bunch of scraps. I wasn't trained on how to build a human being from nothing. Life did train me. I started putting this scrap metal together. Before I knew it, man, I built this beautiful car. I'm like, how did I do that?

paragraphs.speaker: rich
paragraphs.name: Rich Roll
paragraphs.text: The real gift built into this whole thing is how you reframe vulnerability. We tend to think of vulnerability as a weakness or somebody who's soft. But you embody it in a completely different structure to show people that it is strength. If you want to be hard and you want to be strong, vulnerability and open-mindedness are the keys to that kingdom.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Goggins
paragraphs.text: The one thing that made me who I am today is being vulnerable. It's breaking myself down to the absolute rock bottom and being able to tell people who I am. A lot of this isn't your fault why you do some things you do, why you feel the way you feel. But no one's coming back to save your ass. You have to go back to where this shit started and have the courage to go back there and start fixing what broke you.

paragraphs.speaker: rich
paragraphs.name: Rich Roll
paragraphs.text: Let's recap your story for people who are new. You were born in Buffalo. Your dad owned skating rinks and bars, was an alcoholic, used to get women for favors—basically a pimp. The second that door was shut in that house, it was game on. The beatings were horrible, but the mind torture was the worst.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Goggins
paragraphs.text: My dad was a perfect person for psychological warfare. He got so deep in my head as a young kid, and that really fucks up your foundation. When you see your mom getting beat senseless at a young kid, that shit scars you permanently. At eight years old, I started becoming real protective of my mother. Whatever you're going through, man, you're on your own. You're not gonna tell your mom shit. That's where I started really creating myself.

paragraphs.speaker: rich
paragraphs.name: Rich Roll
paragraphs.text: The inciting incident that starts to shift your trajectory is this Air Force guy, Scott Guerin, who survived a parachuting accident—fell 13,000 feet to his death essentially. You met him at Civil Air Patrol camp and became obsessed. Why did it connect with you and not the other kids?

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Goggins
paragraphs.text: I was looking for strength my entire life. I didn't have any.
